verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then returned they unto Jerusalem from the mount called Olivet, which is from Jerusalem a sabbath day’s journey.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then returned they,” it is said: namely, when they had heard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they could not have borne it, if the angel had not (ὑπερέθετο) referred them to another Coming.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me, that it was also on a sabbath-day that these things took place; for he would not thus have specified the distance, saying, “from the mount called Olivet, which is from Jerusalem a sabbath day’s journey,” unless they were then going on the sabbath-day a certain definite distance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come in,” it says, “they went up into an upper room, where they were making their abode:” so they then remained in Jerusalem after the Resurrection: “both Peter, and James, and John:” no longer is only the latter together with his brother mentioned, but together with Peter the two: “and Andrew, and Philip, and Thomas, Bartholomew, and Matthew, and James (the son) of Alphæus, and Simon Zelotes, and Judas, (the brother) of James.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has done well to mention the disciples: for since one had betrayed Christ, and another had been unbelieving, he thereby shows that, except the first, all of them were preserved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “These were all continuing with one accord in prayer together with the women.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is a powerful weapon in temptations; and to this they had been trained. [“Continuing with one accord.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Good. (καλὥς).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, the present temptation directed them to this: for they exceedingly feared the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With the women,” it is said: for he had said that they had followed Him: “and with Mary the mother of Jesus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Luke xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then [is it said, that “that disciple”] took her to his own home” (John xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26), at that time?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then the Lord had brought them together again, and so returned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And with His brethren.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These also were before unbelieving.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And in those days,” it says, “Peter stood up in the midst of the disciples, and said.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both as being ardent, and as having been put in trust by Christ with the flock, and as having precedence in honor, he always begins the discourse. (“The number of the names together were about an hundred and twenty.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Men and brethren,” he says, “this Scripture must needs have been fulfilled, which the Holy Ghost spake before,” [etc.] (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did he not ask Christ to give him some one in the room of Judas?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is better as it is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in the first place, they were engaged in other things; secondly, of Christ’s presence with them, the greatest proof that could be given was this: as He had chosen when He was among them, so did He now being absent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now this was no small matter for their consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe how Peter does everything with the common consent; nothing imperiously.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not speak thus without a meaning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe how he consoles them concerning what had passed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, what had happened had caused them no small consternation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if there are many now who canvass this circumstance, what may we suppose they had to say then?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men and brethren,” says Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the Lord called them brethren, much more may he. [“Men,” he says]: they all being present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See the dignity of the Church, the angelic condition!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No distinction there, “neither male nor female.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would that the Churches were such now!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None there had his mind full of some worldly matter, none was anxiously thinking about household concerns.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such a benefit are temptations, such the advantage of afflictions!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Scripture,” says he, “must needs have been fulfilled, which the Holy Ghost spake before.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Always he comforts them by the prophecies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So does Christ on all occasions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the very same way, he shows here that no strange thing had happened, but what had already been foretold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Scripture must needs have been fulfilled,” he says, “which the Holy Ghost by the mouth of David spake before.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, David, but the Spirit through him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what kind of doctrine the writer has at the very outset of the book.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see, that it was not for nothing that I said in the beginning of this work, that this book is the Polity of the Holy Spirit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which the Holy Ghost spake before by the mouth of David.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he appropriates (οἰκειοὕται) him; and that it is an advantage to them, that this was spoken by David, and not by some other Prophet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Concerning Judas,” he says, “which was guide.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again mark the philosophical temper of the man: how he does not mention him with scorn, nor say, “that wretch,” “that miscreant:” but simply states the fact; and does not even say, “who betrayed Him,” but does what he can to transfer the guilt to others: nor does he animadvert severely even on these: “Which was guide,” he says, “to them that took Jesus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Furthermore, before he declares where David had spoken, he relates what had been the case with Judas, that from the things present he may fetch assurance of the things future, and show that this man had already received his due.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For he was numbered,” says he, “with us, and had obtained part of this ministry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now this man acquired a field out of the reward of iniquity.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He gives his discourse a moral turn, and covertly mentions the cause of the wickedness, because it carried reproof with it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, The Jews, but, “this man, acquired” it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since the minds of weak persons do not attend to things future, as they do to things present, he discourses of the immediate punishment inflicted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And falling headlong, he burst asunder in the midst.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does well to dilate not upon the sin, but upon the punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And,” he says, “all his bowels gushed out.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This brought them consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it was known unto all the dwellers at Jerusalem; insomuch as that field is called in their proper tongue Aceldama, that is to say, the field of blood.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now the Jews gave it this name, not on this account, but because of Judas; here, however, Peter makes it to have this reference, and when he brings forward the adversaries as witnesses, both by the fact that they named it, and by saying, “in their proper tongue,” this is what he means.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then after the event, he appositely brings in the Prophet, saying, “For it is written in the Book of Psalms, Let his habitation be desolate, and let no man dwell therein” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20) (Ps. lxix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25): this is said of the field and the dwelling: “And his bishopric let another take; that is, his office, his priesthood.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that this, he says, is not my counsel, but His who hath foretold these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that he may not seem to be undertaking a great thing, and just such as Christ had done, he adduces the Prophet as a witness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wherefore it behooves of these men which have companied with us all the time.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he make it their business too?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That the matter might not become an object of strife, and they might not fall into contention about it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the Apostles themselves once did this, much more might those.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he ever avoids.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore at the beginning he said, “Men and brethren.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It behooves” to choose from among you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He defers the decision to the whole body, thereby both making the elected objects of reverence and himself keeping clear of all invidiousness with regard to the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such occasions always give rise to great evils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now that some one must needs be appointed, he adduces the prophet as witness: but from among what persons: “Of these,” he says, “which have companied with us all the time.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To have said, the worthy must present themselves, would have been to insult the others; but now he refers the matter to length of time; for he says not simply, “These who have companied with us,” but, “all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us, beginning from the baptism of John unto that same day that He was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a witness with us of His resurrection” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): that their college (ὁ χορὸς) might not be left mutilated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why did it not rest with Peter to make the election himself: what was the motive?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This; that he might not seem to bestow it of favor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, he was not yet endowed with the spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they appointed two, Joseph called Barsabus, who was surnamed Justus, and Matthias.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not he appointed them: but it was he that introduced the proposition to that effect, at the same time pointing out that even this was not his own, but from old time by prophecy; so that he acted as expositor, not as preceptor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Joseph called Barsabus, who was surnamed Justus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps both names are given, because there were others of the same name, for among the Apostles also there were several names alike; as James, and James (the son) of Alphæus; Simon Peter, and Simon Zelotes; Judas (the brother) of James, and Judas Iscariot.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The appellation, however, may have arisen from a change of life, and very likely also of the moral character.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They appointed two,” it is said, “Joseph called Barsabus, who was surnamed Justus, and Matthias.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they prayed, and said; Thou, Lord, which knowest the hearts of all men, show whether of these two thou hast chosen, that he may take part of this ministry and Apostleship, from which Judas by transgression fell, that he might go to his own place.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They do well to mention the sin of Judas, thereby showing that it is a witness they ask to have; not increasing the number, but not suffering it to be diminished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they gave forth their lots” (for the spirit was not yet sent), “and the lot fell upon Matthias: and he was numbered with the eleven Apostles.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then,” it says, “returned they unto Jerusalem from the mount called Olivet (Recapitulation), [“which is nigh to Jerusalem, at the distance of a sabbath-day’s journey:”] so that there was no long way to go, to be a cause of alarm to them while yet trembling and fearful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come in, they went up into an upper room.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They durst not appear in the town.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They also did well to go up into an upper room, as it became less easy to arrest them at once.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they continued,” it is said, “with one accord in prayer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see how watchful they were?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Continuing in prayer,” and “with one accord,” as it were with one soul, continuing therein: two things reported in their praise. [“Where they were abiding,” etc., to, “And Mary the Mother of Jesus and His brethren.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now Joseph perhaps was dead: for it is not to be supposed that when the brethren had become believers, Joseph believed not; he who in fact had believed before any.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Certain it is that we nowhere find him looking upon Christ as man merely.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As where His mother said, [“Thy father and I did seek thee sorrowing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And upon another occasion, it was said,] “Thy mother and thy brethren seek thee.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that Joseph knew this before all others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And to them [the brethren] Christ said, “The world cannot hate you, but Me it hateth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, consider the moderation of James.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He it was who received the Bishopric of Jerusalem, and here he says nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark also the great moderation of the other Apostles, how they concede the throne to him, and no longer dispute with each other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that Church was as it were in heaven: having nothing to do with this world’s affairs: and resplendent not with wails, no, nor with numbers, but with the zeal of them that formed the assembly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were “about an hundred and twenty,” it says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The seventy perhaps whom Christ Himself had chosen, and other of the more earnest-minded disciples, as Joseph and Matthias. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There were women, he says, many, who followed Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 41.) [“The number of the names together.]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Together” they were on all occasions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here is forethought for providing a teacher; here was the first who ordained a teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not say, ‘We are sufficient.’

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far was he beyond all vain-glory, and he looked to one thing alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he had the same power to ordain as they all collectively.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But well might these things be done in this fashion, through the noble spirit of the man, and because prelacy then was not an affair of dignity, but of provident care for the governed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This neither made the elected to become elated, for it was to dangers that they were called, nor those not elected to make a grievance of it, as if they were disgraced.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But things are not done in this fashion now; nay, quite the contrary.—For observe, they were an hundred and twenty, and he asks for one out of the whole body: with good right, as having been put in charge of them: for to him had Christ said, “And when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For he was numbered with us,” (πρὥτος τοῦ πράγματος αὐθεντει absent from A.B.C ) says Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On this account it behooves to propose another; to be a witness in his place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how he imitates his Master, ever discoursing from the Scriptures, and saying nothing as yet concerning Christ; namely, that He had frequently predicted this Himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nor does he mention where the Scripture speaks of the treachery of Judas; for instance, “The mouth of the wicked and the mouth of the deceitful are opened against me” (Ps. cix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1.); but where it speaks only of his punishment; for this was most to their advantage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows again the benevolence of the Lord: “For he was numbered with us” (τοὕτο γὰρ αὐτοὺς μάλιστα ὡφέλει· Δείκνυσι πάλιν A.B.C ), he says, “and obtained his lot of this ministry.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He calls it everywhere “lot,” showing that the whole is from God’s grace and election, and reminding them of the old times, inasmuch as God chose him into His own lot or portion, as of old He took the Levites.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He also dwells upon the circumstances respecting Judas, showing that the reward of the treachery was made itself the herald of the punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he “acquired,” he says, “a field out of the reward of the iniquity.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the divine economy in the event.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Of the iniquity,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are many iniquities, but never was anything more iniquitous than this: so that the affair was one of iniquity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now not only to those who were present did the event become known, but to all thereafter, so that without meaning or knowing what they were about, they gave it a name; just as Caiaphas had prophesied unconsciously.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God compelled them to call the field in Hebrew “Aceldama.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By this also the evils which were to come upon the Jews were declared: and Peter shows the prophecy to have been so far in part fulfilled, which says, “It had been good for that man if he had not been born.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We may with propriety apply this same to the Jews likewise; for if he who was guide suffered thus, much more they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus far however Peter says nothing of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, showing that the term, “Aceldama,” might well be applied to his fate, he introduces the prophet, saying, “Let his habitation be desolate.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what can be worse desolation than to become a place of burial?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the field may well be called his.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he who cast down the price, although others were the buyers, has a right to be himself reckoned owner of a great desolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This desolation was the prelude to that of the Jews, as will appear on looking closely into the facts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed they destroyed themselves by famine, and killed many, and the city became a burial-place of strangers, of soldiers, for as to those, they would not even have let them be buried, for in fact they were not deemed worthy of sepulture.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wherefore of these men which have companied with us,” continues Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how desirous he is they should be eye-witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is true indeed that the Spirit would shortly come; and yet great care is shown with regard to this circumstance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Of these men,” he says, “which have companied with us, all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that they had dwelt with Christ, not simply been present as disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, from the very beginning there were many that then followed Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, for instance, how this appears in these words: “One of the two which heard John speak, and followed Jesus.—All the time,” he says, “that the Lord Jesus went in and out among us, beginning from the baptism of John.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True! for no one knew what preceded that event, though they did learn it by the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Unto that same day that He was taken up from us, must one be ordained to be a witness with us of His resurrection.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He said not, a witness of the rest of his actions, but a witness of the resurrection alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed that witness had a better right to be believed, who was able to declare, that He Who ate and drank, and was crucified, the same rose again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore it was needed that he should be a witness, not only of the time preceding this event, nor only of what followed it, and of the miracles; the thing required was, the resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the other matters were manifest and acknowledged, but the resurrection took place in secret, and was manifest to these only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they do not say, Angels have told us; but, We have seen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this it was that was most needful at that time: that they should be men having a right to be believed, because they had seen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they appointed two,” it is said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why not many?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That the feeling of disappointment might not reach further, extending to many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, it is not without reason that he puts Matthias last; he would show, that frequently he that is honourable among men, is inferior before God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they all pray in common saying, “Thou, Lord, which knowest the hearts of all men, show.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou,” not “We.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And very seasonably they use the epithet, “heart-knowing:” for by Him Who is this must the choice be made.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So confident were they, that assuredly one of them must be appointed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They said not, Choose, but, “Show the chosen one;” knowing that all things were foreordained of God; “Whom Thou didst choose: one of these two,” say they, “to have his lot in this ministry and apostleship.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there was besides another ministry (διακονία).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they gave them their lots.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they did not yet consider themselves to be worthy to be informed by some sign.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, if in a case where neither prayer was made, nor men of worth were the agents, the casting of lots so much availed, because it was done of a right intention, I mean in the case of Jonah (Jonah i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7); much more did it here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, did he, the designated, fill up the company, complete the order: but the other candidate was not annoyed; for the apostolic writers would not have concealed [that or any other] failings of their own, seeing they have told of the very chief Apostles, that on other occasions they had indignation (Matt. xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24; Matt. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8), and this not once only, but again and again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then also imitate them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now I address no longer every one, but those who aim at preferment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou believest that the election is with God, be not displeased.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is with Him thou art displeased, and with Him thou art exasperated: it is He who has made the choice; thou doest the very thing that Cain did; because, forsooth, his brother’s sacrifice was preferred, he was indignant, when he ought to have felt compunction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, that is not what I mean here; but this, that God knows how to dispense things for the best.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In many cases, thou art in point of disposition more estimable than the other but not the fit person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, on the other hand, thy life is irreproachable, and thy habits those of a well-nurtured man, but in the Church this is not all that is wanted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, one man is adapted for one thing, another for another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not observe, how much discourse the holy Scripture has made on this matter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let me say why it is that the thing has become a subject of competition: it is because we come to the Episcopate not as unto a work of governing and superintending the brethren, but as to a post of dignity and repose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did you but know that a Bishop is bound to belong to all, to bear the burden of all; that others, if they are angry, are pardoned, but he never; that others, if they sin, have excuses made for them, he has none; you would not be eager for the dignity, would not run after it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it is, the Bishop is exposed to the tongues of all, to the criticism of all, whether they be wise or fools.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is harassed with cares every day, nay, every night.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has many to hate him, many to envy him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Talk not to me of those who curry favor with all, of those who desire to sleep, of those who advance to this office as for repose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have nothing to do with these; we speak of those who watch for your souls, who consider the safety and welfare of those under them before their own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me now: suppose a man has ten children, always living with him, and constantly under his control; yet is he solicitous about them; and a bishop, who has such numbers, not living under the same roof with him, but owing obedience to his authority—what does he not need to be!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he is honored, you will say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With what sort of honor, indeed!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, the paupers and beggars abuse him openly in the market-place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why does he not stop their mouths then?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, very proper work, this, for a bishop, is it not?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again, if he do not give to all, the idle and the industrious alike, lo! a thousand complaints on all sides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None is afraid to accuse him, and speak evil of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the case of civil governors, fear steps in; with bishops, nothing of the kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As for the fear of God, it does not influence people, as regards them, in the least degree.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why speak of the anxiety connected with the word and doctrine? the painful work in Ordinations?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Either, perhaps, I am a poor wretched incompetent creature, or else, the case is as I say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soul of a Bishop is for all the world like a vessel in a storm: lashed from every side, by friends, by foes, by one’s own people, by strangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does not the Emperor rule the whole world, the Bishop a single city?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet a Bishop’s anxieties are as much beyond those of the emperor, as the waters of a river simply moved, by the wind are surpassed in agitation by the swelling and raging sea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why? because in the one case there are many to lend a hand, for all goes on by law and by rule; but in the other there is none of this, nor is there authority to command; but if one be greatly moved, then he is harsh; if the contrary, then he is cold!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in him these opposites must meet, that he may neither be despised, nor be hated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, the very demands of business preoccupy him: how many is he obliged to offend, whether he will or not!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many to be severe with!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I speak not otherwise than it is, but as I find it in my own actual experience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not think there are many among Bishops that will be saved, but many more that perish: and the reason is, that it is an affair that requires a great mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many are the exigencies which throw a man out of his natural temper; and he had need have a thousand eyes on all sides.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see what a number of qualifications the Bishop must have? to be apt to teach, patient, holding fast the faithful word in doctrine (see 1 Tim. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2–9; Tit. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What trouble and pains does this require!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then, others do wrong, and he bears all the blame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To pass over every thing else: if one soul depart unbaptized, does not this subvert all his own prospect of salvation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The loss of one soul carries with it a penalty which no language can represent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the salvation of that soul was of such value, that the Son of God became man, and suffered so much, think how sore a punishment must the losing of it bring!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if in this present life he who is cause of another’s destruction is worthy of death, much more in the next world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not tell me, that the presbyter is in fault, or the deacon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The guilt of all these comes perforce upon the head of those who ordained them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let me mention another instance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It chances, that a bishop has inherited from his predecessor a set of persons of indifferent character.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What measures is it proper to take in respect of bygone transgressions (for here are two precipices) so as not to let the offender go unpunished, and not to cause scandal to the rest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Must one’s first step be to cut him off?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is no actual present ground for that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But is it right to let him go unmarked?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, say you; for the fault rests with the bishop who ordained him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then? must one refuse to ordain him again, and to raise him to a higher degree of the ministry?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That would be to publish it to all men, that he is a person of indifferent character, and so again one would cause scandal in a different way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But is one to promote him to a higher degree?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is much worse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then there were only the responsibility of the office itself for people to run after in the episcopate, none would be so quick to accept it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as things go, we run after this, just as we do after the dignities of the world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That we may have glory with men, we lose ourselves with God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What profit in such honor?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How self-evident its nothingness is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When you covet the episcopal rank, put in the other scale, the account to be rendered after this life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Weigh against it, the happiness of a life free from toil, take into account the different measure of the punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I mean, that even if you have sinned, but in your own person merely, you will have no such great punishment, nothing like it: but if you have sinned as bishop, you are lost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Remember what Moses endured, what wisdom he displayed, what good deeds he exhibited: but, for committing one sin only, he was bitterly punished; and with good reason; for this fault was attended with injury to the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not in regard that the sin was public, but because it was the sin of a spiritual Ruler (ίερέως) cf.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: S.); for in truth we do not pay the same penalty for public and for hidden faults.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Aug in Ps. xcix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The sin may be the same, but not the (ζημία) harm of it; nay, not the sin itself; for it is not the same thing to sin in secret and unseen, and to sin openly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the bishop cannot sin unobserved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well for him if he escape reproach, though he sin not; much less can he think to escape notice, if he do sin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let him be angry, let him laugh, or let him but dream of a moment’s relaxation, many are they that scoff, many that are offended, many that lay down the law, many that bring to mind the former bishops, and abuse the present one; not that they wish to sound the praise of those; no, it is only to carp at him that they bring up the mention of fellow-bishops, of presbyters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Sweet, says the proverb, is war to the inexperienced; but it may rather be said now, that even after one has come out of it, people in general have seen nothing of it: for in their eyes it is not war, but like those shepherds in Ezekiel, we slay and devour.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ezek. xxxiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which of us has it in his power to show that he has taken as much care for the flocks of Christ, as Jacob did for Laban’s?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which of us can tell of the frost of the night?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For talk not to me of vigils, and all that parade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The contrary plainly is the fact.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Prefects, and governors (ὕπαρχοι καὶ τοπάρχαι) of provinces, do not enjoy such honour as he that governs the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he enter the palace, who but he is first?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he go to see ladies, or visit the houses of the great, none is preferred to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The whole state of things is ruined and corrupt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not speak thus as wishing to put us bishops to shame, but to repress your hankering after the office.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For with what conscience, (even should you succeed in becoming a bishop, having made interest for it either in person or by another), with what eyes will you look the man in the face who worked with you to that end?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What will you have to plead for your excuse?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that unwillingly, by compulsion and not with his own consent, was raised to the office, may have something to say for himself, though for the most part even such an one has no pardon to expect, and yet truly he so far has something to plead in excuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think how it fared with Simon Magus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What signifies it that you give not money, if, in place of money, you pay court, you lay many plans, you set engines to work?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thy money perish with thee!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus was it said to him, and thus will it be said to these: your canvassing perish with you, because you have thought to purchase the gift of God by human intrigue!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But there is none such here?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And God forbid there should be!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not that I wish any thing of what I have been saying to be applicable to you: but just now the connexion has led us on to these topics.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In like manner when we talk against covetousness, we are not preaching at you, no, nor against any one man personally.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God grant it may be the case, that these remedies were prepared by us without necessity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The wish of the physician is, that after all his pains, his drugs may be thrown away because not wanted: and this is just what we desire, that our words may not have been needed, and so have been spoken to the wind, so as to be but words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am ready to submit to anything, rather than be reduced to the necessity of using this language.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if you like, we are ready to leave off; only let our silence be without bad effects.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No one, I imagine, though he were ever so vainglorious, would wish to make a display of severity, when there is nothing to call for it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I will leave the teaching to you: for that is the best teaching, which teaches by actions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed the best physicians, although the sickness of their patients brings them in fees, would rather their friends were well.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And so we too wish all to be well.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Cor. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not that we desire to be approved, and you reproved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would gladly manifest, if it were possible, with my very eyes, the love which I bear to you: for then no one would be able to reproach me, though my language were ever so rough.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For speech of friends, yea, were it insult, can be borne;” more “faithful are the wounds of a friend, rather than the ready kisses of an enemy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Prov. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There nothing I love more than you, no, not even light itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would gladly have my eyes put out ten thousand times over, if it were possible by this means to convert your souls; so much is your salvation dearer to me than light itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what profit to me in the rays of the sun, when despondency on your account makes it all thick darkness before my eyes?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Light is good when it shines in cheerfulness, to a sorrowful heart it seems even to be a trouble.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How true this is, may you never learn by experience!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, if it happen to any of you to fall into sin, just stand by my bedside, when I am laid down to rest and should be asleep; see whether I am not like a palsied man, like one beside himself, and, in the language of the prophet, “the light of mine eyes, it also is gone from me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. xxxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For where is our hope, if you do not make progress? where our despondency, if you do excellently?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I seem to have wings, when I hear any thing good of you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Fulfil ye my joy.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This one thing is the burden of my prayers, that I long for your advancement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that in which I strive with all is this, that I love you, that I am wrapped up in you, that you are my all, father, mother, brethren, children.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think not then that any thing that has been said was said in a hostile spirit, nay, it is for your amendment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is written “A brother assisted by his brother is as a strong city.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Prov. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then do not take it in disdain: for neither do I undervalue what you have to say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I should wish even to be set right by you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For all (Edd. ‘all we’) ye are brethren, and One is our Master: yet even among brothers it is for one to direct, while the others obey.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then disdain it not, but let us do all to the glory of God, for to Him belongs glory for ever and ever.
